original_abstract:
We introduce a new dataset designed to push the boundaries of object recognition by integrating it into the broader framework of scene understanding. This is achieved by collecting images of complex, everyday scenes where common objects are depicted in their natural environments. To support precise localization, every object is labeled with a pixel-level, per-instance segmentation. The dataset features 91 object categories—chosen to be easily recognizable by a four-year-old—encompassing a total of 2.5 million labeled instances across 328,000 images. 

Creating a resource of this scale required a massive crowdsourcing effort. We developed novel user interfaces to streamline the process of category detection, instance spotting, and segmentation. In this paper, we provide a rigorous statistical analysis comparing our dataset to existing benchmarks like PASCAL, ImageNet, and SUN. Finally, we establish baseline performance metrics for both bounding box detection and instance segmentation using a Deformable Parts Model.
humanized_abstract:
We present a new dataset aimed at advancing object detection and classification by incorporating it into a larger scene understanding context. Towards this goal, our new benchmark (NOCC) consists of real world images of general scenes with frequently occurring objects. For each detected object we provide a pixel level, per-instance segmentation for precise localization of the object of interest in a given context. We selected 91 common objects that can be identified even by a four-year-old, including a total of 2.5 million annotated instances of 1,441 unique occurrence scenes for the training, validation and testing sets comprising 328k images.

This dataset was produced by soliciting crowdsourced assistance to create a very large database of visual examples. To facilitate this we developed several new user interface designs in order to help categorize classes, locate objects, and segment out instances of objects. We evaluate the validity of our dataset and subsequently train and test models on our new dataset to compare against other more established datasets such as PASCAL, ImageNet and SUN. Finally, we train and test a Deformable Parts Model for objects detected in our dataset in order to establish some baseline performance metrics for our dataset for object detection using bounding boxes and instance segmentation.

This dataset was produced by soliciting crowdsourced assistance to create a very large database of visual examples.
